title: VPC & Networking Foundation
description:
## Summary
Provision the foundational AWS VPC and networking infrastructure that all other AWS resources depend on.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- VPC with public/private/database subnets across 2-3 AZs
- fck-nat instance for private subnet egress (cost-optimized)
- Internet gateway for public subnets
- Route tables (public, private, database)
- Security groups (baseline, can be extended by dependent epics)
- S3 Gateway Endpoint (free) for ECR layers and logs

## Subnet Tiers
| Tier | Purpose | Internet Access |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| Public | ALB, NAT | Direct (IGW) |
| Private | EKS pods, apps | Outbound only (NAT) |
| Database | RDS, Redis | None (isolated) |

## Key Decisions
- CIDR block sizing: /16 VPC with /20 subnets (4k IPs per subnet)
- AZ count: 2 for cost efficiency (can expand to 3 later)
- NAT: fck-nat on t4g.nano (~$3/mo) instead of NAT Gateway (~$32/mo)
- VPC Endpoints: S3 gateway only (free). Skip interface endpoints (~$7/mo each) - use fck-nat instead.
- DB access: Use kubectl exec into pods, no bastion needed

## Deferred (add later if needed)
- **CloudFront**: Not needed initially. Easy to add in front of ALB later.
- **VPC Flow Logs**: Enable when debugging network issues. ~$0.50/GB to CloudWatch.
- **Route53**: DNS managed externally. ALB DNS name output is sufficient for CNAME setup.
- **Bastion/VPN**: Use kubectl exec or debug pods for DB access instead.
- **Tagging strategy**: Basic environment tags for now. Per-customer resource tagging will be important later for cost allocation.

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/`
- Environment configs: dev, staging, prod
- Outputs: VPC ID, subnet IDs (public, private, database), security group IDs, ALB DNS name

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for overall AWS topology
- fck-nat: https://fck-nat.dev/

title: Create VPC Terraform module structure
description:
Set up base Terraform module at infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/ with main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, versions.tf. Define input variables for environment, region, CIDR block, AZ count.

**Done when:**
- [ ] Directory structure exists: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/`
- [ ] Files created: main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, versions.tf
- [ ] Variables defined: environment, region, vpc_cidr, az_count
- [ ] `terraform validate` passes

title: Implement VPC with public/private/database subnets
description:
Create VPC with /16 CIDR block. Implement three subnet tiers across 2 AZs (expandable to 3):

- **Public subnets** (/20, ~4k IPs): ALB, NAT instance
- **Private subnets** (/20, ~4k IPs): EKS Fargate pods, application workloads
- **Database subnets** (/20, ~4k IPs): RDS, ElastiCache - fully isolated, no NAT route

Database subnets have no route to internet (inbound or outbound). Only accessible from private subnets via security groups.

Use data source for available AZs.

**Done when:**
- [ ] VPC created with /16 CIDR
- [ ] 2 public subnets (one per AZ)
- [ ] 2 private subnets (one per AZ)
- [ ] 2 database subnets (one per AZ)
- [ ] `terraform plan` shows expected resources
- [ ] Subnets tagged with tier (public/private/database)

title: Implement Internet Gateway and NAT Gateways
description:
Create Internet Gateway for public subnets. Deploy fck-nat instance (t4g.nano) for NAT instead of NAT Gateway to minimize costs (~$3/mo vs ~$32/mo). Single NAT instance for all environments initially - can upgrade to NAT Gateway later if reliability becomes critical. Use Elastic IP for the NAT instance. Reference: https://fck-nat.dev/

**Done when:**
- [ ] Internet Gateway created and attached to VPC
- [ ] Elastic IP allocated for NAT
- [ ] fck-nat instance launched in public subnet with EIP
- [ ] NAT instance can reach internet (test: curl from instance)

title: Implement route tables for public and private subnets
description:
Create route tables for each subnet tier:

- **Public route table**: Default route (0.0.0.0/0) to Internet Gateway. Associate with public subnets.
- **Private route table**: Default route to fck-nat instance. Associate with private subnets.
- **Database route table**: No default route (fully isolated). Associate with database subnets.

Database tier has no outbound internet path - RDS/Redis can only be reached from within the VPC.

**Done when:**
- [ ] Public route table with 0.0.0.0/0 → IGW
- [ ] Private route table with 0.0.0.0/0 → fck-nat ENI
- [ ] Database route table with local routes only
- [ ] Each subnet associated with correct route table

title: Implement baseline security groups (Fargate model)
description:
Create baseline security groups for Fargate-based EKS (no EC2 nodes):

- **ALB SG**: Inbound 80/443 from 0.0.0.0/0, outbound to EKS Pod SG
- **EKS Pod SG**: Inbound from ALB SG on app ports, outbound to RDS SG, Redis SG, and NAT SG. Attached to Fargate pod ENIs via EKS SecurityGroupPolicy.
- **RDS SG**: Inbound 5432 from EKS Pod SG only
- **Redis SG**: Inbound 6379 from EKS Pod SG only
- **NAT SG**: Inbound all traffic from private subnet CIDRs (for outbound internet via fck-nat)

Note: Fargate pods get individual ENIs with VPC IPs - no "node" security group needed. The EKS Pod SG is applied to pod ENIs, not EC2 instances.

**Done when:**
- [ ] All 5 security groups created
- [ ] Rules reference other SGs by ID (not CIDR) where applicable
- [ ] Security group IDs exported as outputs

title: Implement S3 Gateway Endpoint
description:
Create S3 Gateway Endpoint (free) for ECR image layers, logs, and Terraform state. Skip interface endpoints (ECR API, CloudWatch, STS, Secrets Manager) - let this traffic go through fck-nat instead. Interface endpoints cost ~$7/mo/AZ each which defeats the cost savings from fck-nat.

Future consideration: Add interface endpoints if fck-nat becomes a bottleneck or if security requirements mandate traffic stay within VPC.

**Done when:**
- [ ] S3 Gateway Endpoint created
- [ ] Endpoint associated with private and database route tables
- [ ] S3 traffic from private subnets does not go through NAT (verify in route table)

title: Create environment configs (dev, staging, prod)
description:
Create tfvars files for dev, staging, prod environments. All environments use single fck-nat instance initially (upgrade to NAT Gateway per-AZ for prod later if needed).

**Environment configs:**
- dev.tfvars: 2 AZs, single NAT
- staging.tfvars: 2 AZs, single NAT  
- prod.tfvars: 2 AZs, single NAT (upgrade path documented)

**Done when:**
- [ ] tfvars files created for dev, staging, prod
- [ ] `terraform plan -var-file=dev.tfvars` succeeds
- [ ] Outputs exported: vpc_id, public_subnet_ids, private_subnet_ids, database_subnet_ids, alb_sg_id, eks_pod_sg_id, rds_sg_id, redis_sg_id

title: Bootstrap Terraform state backend
description:
Create S3 bucket and DynamoDB table for Terraform remote state before any other infrastructure.

**Resources:**
- S3 bucket: `<project>-terraform-state` with versioning enabled
- DynamoDB table: `terraform-locks` with PAY_PER_REQUEST billing, LockID (string) as hash key

**Bootstrap process:**
1. Create `infrastructure/terraform/bootstrap/` with state backend resources
2. Apply locally (state stored in git initially, one-time only)
3. Configure backend in `infrastructure/terraform/modules/` to use remote state
4. All subsequent terraform runs use S3 + DynamoDB

**S3 bucket settings:**
- Versioning: enabled (recover from bad applies)
- Encryption: AES-256 (SSE-S3)
- Public access: blocked
- Lifecycle: keep all versions (state history)

**Done when:**
- [ ] S3 bucket exists with versioning enabled
- [ ] DynamoDB table exists with LockID key
- [ ] `terraform init` in modules/ configures remote backend successfully
- [ ] `terraform plan` acquires and releases lock

title: Cognito SAML Integration
description:
## Summary
Configure Cognito for SAML-based authentication with enterprise identity providers (university SSO systems).

## Scope
- SAML Identity Provider configuration in Cognito
- Attribute mapping from SAML assertions to ID token claims
- Sign-in/sign-out flow configuration
- Testing with a mock SAML IdP

## SAML Attribute Mapping Strategy
Map standard attributes to Cognito built-in attributes:
- `email` -> `email`
- `given_name` -> `given_name`
- `family_name` -> `family_name`

**IdP-specific attributes** (eduPersonAffiliation, eppn, department, etc.) should NOT be mapped to Cognito custom attributes. Instead:
1. Configure Cognito to pass unmapped SAML claims through in the ID token
2. Go API captures these claims on first login
3. Application database stores claims per-tenant with configurable mapping

This approach keeps Cognito schema-agnostic and supports universities with different attribute schemas.

## Flows to Support
1. SP-initiated SSO (user clicks "Login with SSO" -> redirect to IdP)
2. IdP-initiated SSO (user starts at IdP, lands at app)
3. Single Logout (SLO) if supported by IdP

## Deliverables
- Terraform configuration for SAML IdP in Cognito
- Documentation on claim passthrough configuration
- Mock SAML IdP setup for local development (e.g., samlidp)
- Integration test scripts

## Dependencies
- Requires: Cognito User Pool Infrastructure (PLAT-dar)

## Related Work
- Go API must implement first-login claim capture (separate task)
- Database schema needs user_identity_claims table (separate task)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 2: Auth + Database Schema
- AWS Cognito SAML documentation

title: Database Schema & Row-Level Security
description:
## Summary
Design and implement the PostgreSQL database schema with Row-Level Security (RLS) policies for multi-tenant isolation.

## Scope
- Complete database schema (tables, indexes, constraints)
- RLS policies using PostgreSQL session variables
- Database migrations tooling (golang-migrate or similar)
- Seed data for development/testing

## Core Tables (based on existing app)
- `namespaces` - multi-tenant organizations
- `users` - user accounts (linked to Cognito)
- `classes` - courses
- `sections` - class sections
- `problems` - assignment problems
- `sessions` - live coding sessions
- `invitations` - user invitations

## RLS Implementation
```sql
-- Example RLS policy pattern
ALTER TABLE classes ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON classes
  USING (namespace_id = current_setting('app.namespace_id', true)::uuid);
```

Session variables set by middleware:
- `app.user_id` - current user UUID
- `app.namespace_id` - current tenant UUID
- `app.role` - user role for permission checks

## Deliverables
- SQL migration files: `go-backend/migrations/`
- Schema documentation
- RLS policy test suite
- Development seed data script

## Dependencies
- Requires: RDS PostgreSQL Database (PLAT-etu)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 2: Auth + Database Schema
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for RLS middleware pattern

title: Go API Project Scaffolding
description:
## Summary
Establish the Go API project structure, configuration management, and build/deployment pipeline. Uses Chi router with go-playground/validator.

## Scope
- Project directory structure per `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`
- Chi router setup with middleware chain
- Configuration management (env vars, feature flags)
- Request binding and validation helpers using go-playground/validator
- Dockerfile and container build
- Kubernetes deployment manifests
- CI/CD pipeline configuration

## Project Structure
\`\`\`
go-backend/
├── cmd/server/main.go       # Entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── config/              # Configuration loading
│   ├── server/              # HTTP server setup, Chi router
│   ├── middleware/          # Logging, recovery, request ID
│   └── handler/             # HTTP handlers (initially just health)
├── pkg/                     # Shared libraries
├── migrations/              # SQL migrations
├── Dockerfile
└── go.mod
\`\`\`

## Framework Choice: Chi
- Minimal, stdlib-compatible router
- No framework lock-in (standard http.Handler interface)
- Explicit middleware composition

## Validation Helper Pattern
\`\`\`go
var validate = validator.New()

func BindJSON[T any](r *http.Request) (T, error) {
    var req T
    if err := json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&req); err != nil {
        return req, err
    }
    return req, validate.Struct(req)
}
\`\`\`

## Deliverables
- Complete project skeleton with working health endpoint
- Docker build and local run instructions
- Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, Service, Ingress)
- CI pipeline (build, test, push to ECR)
- Makefile for common tasks

## Dependencies
- Requires: EKS Compute Platform (PLAT-b3l) - for deployment target

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for Go API structure
- Chi router: github.com/go-chi/chi/v5
- Validator: github.com/go-playground/validator/v10

title: ElastiCache Redis Cluster
description:
## Summary
Provision ElastiCache Redis for caching, rate limiting, and Centrifugo pub/sub backend.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- ElastiCache Redis cluster (cluster mode disabled for simplicity)
- Subnet group (private subnets)
- Security group (allow access from EKS only)
- Parameter group with appropriate settings

## Configuration
- Node type: cache.t3.micro (dev), cache.r6g.large (prod)
- Replicas: 0 for dev, 2 for prod (Multi-AZ)
- Encryption: In-transit and at-rest
- Eviction policy: volatile-lru

## Use Cases
- Centrifugo pub/sub backend (channel presence, message routing)
- Rate limiting (sliding window counters)
- Session caching (optional, JWTs are stateless)
- API response caching (optional)

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/elasticache/`
- Outputs: Primary endpoint, reader endpoint, port

## Dependencies
- Requires: VPC & Networking Foundation (PLAT-03e)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for caching strategy

title: SES Email Identity Setup
description:
## Summary
Configure AWS SES for Cognito email delivery. The default Cognito email limit (50/day) is too low for testing.

## Scope
- Verify domain in SES (e.g., `example.com` or `mail.example.com`)
- Add DNS records: TXT (verification), DKIM (3 CNAME records)
- Request production access (exit sandbox mode)
- Create Terraform resource for SES identity

## Terraform
```hcl
resource "aws_ses_domain_identity" "main" {
  domain = var.email_domain
}

resource "aws_ses_domain_dkim" "main" {
  domain = aws_ses_domain_identity.main.domain
}

# Output the DNS records that need to be added
output "ses_verification_token" {
  value = aws_ses_domain_identity.main.verification_token
}

output "ses_dkim_tokens" {
  value = aws_ses_domain_dkim.main.dkim_tokens
}

output "ses_identity_arn" {
  value = aws_ses_domain_identity.main.arn
}
```

## Manual Steps
1. Apply Terraform to get DNS records
2. Add records to DNS provider
3. Wait for verification (usually minutes)
4. Request production access via AWS console (Support > Service limit increase > SES sending limits)

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/ses/` (or inline in environment config)
- Verified domain identity
- Production access (out of sandbox)
- ARN output for Cognito module to consume

## Notes
SES is simple - this is mostly DNS verification and a support request. No complex configuration needed.

title: Go API Health & Observability
description:
## Summary
Implement health checks, structured logging, and metrics for the Go API to enable monitoring and debugging.

## Scope
- Health check endpoints (liveness, readiness)
- Structured JSON logging with request context
- Prometheus metrics endpoint
- Request tracing with correlation IDs
- Error tracking integration (optional: Sentry)

## Health Endpoints
\`\`\`
GET /healthz        # Liveness - always 200 if process is running
GET /readyz         # Readiness - checks DB, Redis, dependencies
GET /readyz?verbose # Detailed component status
\`\`\`

Readiness checks:
- Database connection pool health
- Redis connectivity
- Cognito JWKS reachable (cached)

## Structured Logging
\`\`\`go
// Using slog (Go 1.21+ stdlib)
slog.Info("request completed",
    "method", r.Method,
    "path", r.URL.Path,
    "status", status,
    "duration_ms", duration.Milliseconds(),
    "request_id", middleware.GetReqID(r.Context()),
    "user_id", user.ID,
)
\`\`\`

## Metrics
Prometheus metrics at `/metrics`:
- `http_requests_total{method, path, status}` - request counter
- `http_request_duration_seconds{method, path}` - latency histogram
- `db_pool_connections{state}` - connection pool gauge
- `db_query_duration_seconds` - query latency histogram

## Request Tracing
- Generate request ID if not present (X-Request-ID header)
- Propagate through all logs and downstream calls
- Include in error responses for debugging

## Deliverables
- \`internal/handler/health.go\` - health endpoints
- \`internal/middleware/logging.go\` - request logging
- \`internal/middleware/metrics.go\` - Prometheus instrumentation
- \`internal/middleware/requestid.go\` - correlation ID handling
- Grafana dashboard JSON (optional)

## Dependencies
- Requires: Go API Project Scaffolding (PLAT-4zm)

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for observability requirements
- Prometheus Go client: github.com/prometheus/client_golang

title: EKS Compute Platform
description:
## Summary
Provision EKS cluster with Fargate, ECR container registry, and ALB ingress controller. This is the compute foundation for all application workloads.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- EKS cluster with Fargate profiles (no EC2 nodes to manage)
- ECR repositories for application images (api, executor, centrifugo)
- ECR lifecycle policies (keep last 10 images, expire untagged after 7 days)
- ALB Ingress Controller for external traffic routing
- IAM roles and OIDC provider for service accounts
- Kubernetes namespaces (app, monitoring, keda)

## Fargate Profiles
- `default` - api workloads
- `executor` - code execution pods (isolated)
- `system` - CoreDNS, ingress controller

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/eks/`
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/ecr/`
- kubectl/helm configs for ALB ingress controller
- Outputs: Cluster endpoint, OIDC provider ARN, ECR repo URLs

## Dependencies
- Requires: VPC & Networking Foundation (PLAT-03e)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for EKS topology

title: Cognito User Pool Infrastructure
description:
## Summary
Provision AWS Cognito user pool and app client for authentication. This is the base infrastructure - SAML configuration is a separate epic.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- Cognito User Pool with appropriate password policies
- User Pool App Client (for the Go API)
- User Pool Domain (for hosted UI, needed for SAML flows)
- IAM roles for Cognito

## Configuration
- Password policy: 12+ chars, mixed case, numbers, symbols
- MFA: Optional (can enable per-tenant later)
- Email verification: Required
- Username: Email-based
- Token validity: Access 1hr, Refresh 30 days

## Architecture Decision: Cognito for Authentication Only
Cognito handles authentication ("who is this person?") but NOT authorization or IdP-specific profile data.

**What Cognito stores:** sub, email, email_verified, name (standard attributes only)

**What Cognito does NOT store:** University-specific claims (affiliation, eppn, department, etc.)

**Why:** Different universities have different identity schemas. Hardcoding custom attributes in Cognito locks the schema after first user and limits multi-tenant flexibility.

**Instead:** IdP claims pass through in the ID token at login. The Go API captures these on first login and stores them in the application database with per-tenant configurable mappings.

See PLAT-29h for SAML claim passthrough configuration.

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/cognito/`
- Outputs: User Pool ID, App Client ID, Domain URL
- Documentation for local development auth flow

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 2: Auth + Database Schema
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for auth flow overview

title: Create Cognito Terraform module structure
description: Set up the base Terraform module structure at infrastructure/terraform/modules/cognito/ with main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, and versions.tf files.

title: Implement Cognito User Pool with password policies
description:
Create the aws_cognito_user_pool resource with: password policy (12+ chars, mixed case, numbers, symbols), email verification required, email-based usernames, optional MFA. Token validity: access 1hr, refresh 30 days.

## Email Configuration
Configure User Pool to use SES (depends on PLAT-7x6):
```hcl
email_configuration {
  email_sending_account = "DEVELOPER"  # Use SES, not Cognito default
  source_arn            = var.ses_identity_arn
  from_email_address    = var.email_from_address  # e.g., noreply@example.com
}
```

Required variables (no defaults):
- `ses_identity_arn` - ARN from SES module
- `email_from_address` - Verified sender address

## Custom Attributes
Do NOT define custom attributes for SAML claims (affiliation, eppn, etc). IdP-specific claims will pass through in the ID token at login and be stored in the application database per-tenant. This keeps Cognito schema-agnostic for multi-tenant flexibility.

title: Implement User Pool App Client
description:
Create aws_cognito_user_pool_client for the Go API backend.

## OAuth Configuration
- **Grant types:** `authorization_code` only (implicit disabled)
- **Scopes:** `openid`, `email`, `profile`
- **Client type:** Confidential (with client secret)
- **PKCE:** Supported automatically if client sends code_challenge/code_verifier (no config needed)

## Callback URLs (required variables, no defaults)
```hcl
variable "callback_urls" {
  description = "Allowed callback URLs after authentication"
  type        = list(string)
}

variable "logout_urls" {
  description = "Allowed URLs after logout"
  type        = list(string)
}
```

Each environment must provide explicit values via tfvars:
- Production: `https://app.example.com/auth/callback`
- Staging: `https://staging.example.com/auth/callback`  
- Dev: Can include `http://localhost:3000/auth/callback`

## Token Settings
- Access token validity: 1 hour
- ID token validity: 1 hour
- Refresh token validity: 30 days

## Notes
- Do NOT enable `implicit` grant (deprecated, insecure)
- Do NOT enable `aws.cognito.signin.user.admin` scope (not managing users via Cognito APIs)
- PKCE can be used by future SPA/mobile clients without config changes
- If a public client (no secret) is needed later, create a separate App Client

title: Implement User Pool Domain
description: Create aws_cognito_user_pool_domain for hosted UI. This is required for SAML flows (covered in separate epic).

title: Implement IAM roles for Cognito
description:
## Summary
Create IAM roles and policies required for Cognito operations.

## Scope
Minimal IAM configuration - no Identity Pool needed (Go API mediates all AWS access).

## Required Roles

### Cognito SES Sending Role (if needed)
Cognito may need a role to send emails via SES. However, this is typically handled via SES resource-based policies rather than Cognito IAM roles.

Check if SES identity policy allows Cognito:
```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Principal": {
      "Service": "cognito-idp.amazonaws.com"
    },
    "Action": "ses:SendEmail",
    "Resource": "arn:aws:ses:REGION:ACCOUNT:identity/example.com"
  }]
}
```

If resource-based policy works, no IAM role needed here.

## NOT Needed
- **Identity Pool roles** - Frontend doesn't access AWS directly; Go API mediates all AWS access via its own EKS/Fargate role
- **Unauthenticated roles** - No anonymous AWS access required

## Deliverables
- Verify SES policy allows Cognito sending (may be handled in PLAT-7x6)
- Document any IAM roles if required
- If no roles needed, document that explicitly and close task

title: Configure Cognito email templates
description:
## Summary
Configure basic email templates for Cognito verification and password reset emails.

## Templates to Configure

### Verification Email
- Subject: "Verify your email"
- Body: Include `{####}` code placeholder, brief instructions

### Password Reset Email  
- Subject: "Reset your password"
- Body: Include `{####}` code placeholder, brief instructions

## Terraform
```hcl
verification_message_template {
  default_email_option = "CONFIRM_WITH_CODE"
  email_subject        = "Verify your email for [App Name]"
  email_message        = "Your verification code is {####}"
}

# Account recovery uses admin_create_user_config for invite emails
# and uses the verification template for forgot password
```

## Notes
- Keep templates simple (plain text) initially
- HTML templates require Lambda trigger (future enhancement if needed)
- Templates are configured in the User Pool resource (dar.2), but separated as a task for clarity
- Depends on SES being configured (PLAT-7x6) for custom from address

title: Validate Cognito setup
description:
## Summary
Verify the Cognito infrastructure works end-to-end after Terraform apply.

## Manual Validation Checklist
1. [ ] Hosted UI loads at User Pool domain URL
2. [ ] Can create test user (AWS Console or CLI)
3. [ ] Verification email sent successfully (check SES metrics)
4. [ ] Can complete verification flow
5. [ ] Can authenticate and receive tokens
6. [ ] JWT contains expected claims (`sub`, `email`, `email_verified`)
7. [ ] Token validity matches configuration (1hr access, 30d refresh)

## CLI Validation Commands
```bash
# Get hosted UI URL
aws cognito-idp describe-user-pool --user-pool-id $POOL_ID --query 'UserPool.Domain'

# Create test user (bypasses email)
aws cognito-idp admin-create-user \
  --user-pool-id $POOL_ID \
  --username test@example.com \
  --temporary-password 'TempPass123!' \
  --message-action SUPPRESS  # Skip welcome email

# Confirm user (bypasses verification)
aws cognito-idp admin-confirm-sign-up \
  --user-pool-id $POOL_ID \
  --username test@example.com

# Set permanent password
aws cognito-idp admin-set-user-password \
  --user-pool-id $POOL_ID \
  --username test@example.com \
  --password 'TestPass123!@#' \
  --permanent

# Get tokens (for testing)
aws cognito-idp admin-initiate-auth \
  --user-pool-id $POOL_ID \
  --client-id $CLIENT_ID \
  --auth-flow ADMIN_USER_PASSWORD_AUTH \
  --auth-parameters USERNAME=test@example.com,PASSWORD='TestPass123!@#'
```

## Deliverables
- Document validation results
- Clean up test users after validation
- Note any issues discovered

## Future: Automated Testing
See task description notes on automated auth testing strategies.

title: RDS PostgreSQL Database
description:
## Summary
Provision RDS PostgreSQL instance for the application database. This provides the persistent data layer.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- RDS PostgreSQL 15+ instance
- DB subnet group (private subnets only)
- Security group (allow access from EKS only)
- Parameter group with RLS-friendly settings
- Automated backups configuration
- Optional: Read replica for prod

## Configuration
- Instance class: db.t3.medium (dev), db.r6g.large (prod)
- Storage: 20GB gp3, autoscaling to 100GB
- Multi-AZ: No for dev, Yes for prod
- Backup retention: 7 days dev, 30 days prod
- Encryption: At rest (AWS-managed keys)

## Connection Management
- Store credentials in AWS Secrets Manager
- Connection string format for Go app
- Max connections sizing for connection pooling

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/rds/`
- Outputs: Endpoint, port, Secrets Manager ARN
- Bastion/jump box config for admin access (or SSM Session Manager)

## Dependencies
- Requires: VPC & Networking Foundation (PLAT-03e)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for data layer design

title: Go API Database Layer
description:
## Summary
Implement database connectivity with connection pooling, transactions, and RLS context middleware for the Go API.

## Scope
- PostgreSQL connection pool (pgx or database/sql with pgx driver)
- Connection configuration from environment/Secrets Manager
- Transaction management utilities
- RLS context middleware (sets session variables)
- Repository pattern base types

## Connection Pool
- Use pgxpool for connection pooling
- Configure pool size based on environment
- Health check with pool stats
- Graceful shutdown

## RLS Context Middleware
\`\`\`go
func RLSContextMiddleware(pool *pgxpool.Pool) func(http.Handler) http.Handler {
    return func(next http.Handler) http.Handler {
        return http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
            user := auth.UserFromContext(r.Context())
            
            // Acquire connection and set session variables
            conn, _ := pool.Acquire(r.Context())
            defer conn.Release()
            
            conn.Exec(r.Context(), "SELECT set_config('app.user_id', $1, true)", user.ID)
            conn.Exec(r.Context(), "SELECT set_config('app.namespace_id', $1, true)", user.NamespaceID)
            
            // Attach conn to context for handlers
            ctx := context.WithValue(r.Context(), dbConnKey, conn)
            next.ServeHTTP(w, r.WithContext(ctx))
        })
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Transaction Utilities
\`\`\`go
func WithTx(ctx context.Context, fn func(tx pgx.Tx) error) error
\`\`\`

## Deliverables
- \`internal/db/pool.go\` - connection pool setup
- \`internal/db/tx.go\` - transaction utilities
- \`internal/middleware/rls.go\` - RLS context middleware
- \`internal/repository/base.go\` - base repository type
- Integration tests with RLS verification

## Dependencies
- Requires: Go API Project Scaffolding (PLAT-4zm)
- Requires: Database Schema & Row-Level Security (PLAT-49o)

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for RLS middleware pattern
- pgx documentation: github.com/jackc/pgx/v5

title: Go API Auth Layer
description:
## Summary
Implement authentication middleware for JWT validation and RBAC authorization in the Go API.

## Scope
- Cognito JWT validation middleware
- JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) fetching and caching
- User context extraction from validated tokens
- Role-based access control (RBAC) middleware
- Permission checking utilities

## JWT Validation
- Fetch and cache Cognito JWKS (refresh on key rotation)
- Validate token signature, issuer, audience, expiry
- Extract claims: sub, email, cognito:groups, custom attributes

## User Context
\`\`\`go
type User struct {
    ID          uuid.UUID
    Email       string
    NamespaceID uuid.UUID
    Role        Role  // admin, instructor, ta, student
    Groups      []string
}

func UserFromContext(ctx context.Context) *User
\`\`\`

## RBAC Middleware
\`\`\`go
// Require specific roles for route groups
r.Group(func(r chi.Router) {
    r.Use(RequireRole(RoleInstructor, RoleAdmin))
    r.Post("/classes", createClass)
})
\`\`\`

## Deliverables
- \`internal/auth/\` package with JWT validation
- \`internal/middleware/auth.go\` - authentication middleware
- \`internal/middleware/rbac.go\` - authorization middleware
- Unit tests with mock tokens
- Integration tests against Cognito (dev environment)

## Dependencies
- Requires: Go API Project Scaffolding (PLAT-4zm)
- Requires: Cognito SAML Integration (PLAT-29h)

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for auth middleware design
- AWS Cognito JWT structure documentation

title: E2E Auth Testing Infrastructure
description:
## Summary
Set up infrastructure for end-to-end testing of registration and authentication flows, including email verification.

## Scope
- Email receiving service for tests to read verification codes
- Test utilities for Cognito user lifecycle
- E2E tests covering: registration → email verification → login → token refresh

## Email Service Options (verify free tiers)

| Service | Free Tier | Notes |
|---------|-----------|-------|
| **Mailtrap** | 100 emails/month | Likely sufficient for E2E |
| **testmail.app** | 100 emails/month | Simple API |
| **Mailosaur** | Free trial only | No permanent free tier (~$10/mo) |
| **Ethereal** | Unlimited | Fake SMTP, good for dev, not real delivery |

**Recommendation:** Start with Mailtrap or testmail.app (free tier), switch if limits are hit.

## Test Cases
1. **Registration flow:** Sign up → receive email → extract code → verify → login
2. **Password reset:** Forgot password → receive email → extract code → reset → login
3. **Token refresh:** Login → wait → refresh token → verify still authenticated

## Implementation Notes
- Use Cognito Admin APIs for test setup/teardown (create users, clean up)
- Email service only needed for the `extract verification code` step
- Consider running full E2E sparingly (nightly) vs admin-bypass tests in CI

## Dependencies
- Requires working Cognito setup (PLAT-dar)
- Requires Go API auth endpoints (PLAT-nqj)

## Future Work
- SAML flow testing (requires mock IdP)
